content.text: Speculative decoding with a draft model is a technique that accelerates LLM generation without changing the output distribution. A small, fast
proposes K tokens ahead, and the large

verifies all K proposals in a single parallel forward pass. Accepted tokens are output immediately; rejected tokens are replaced by the target model's correction. The speedup comes from the fact that verifying K tokens in one pass is barely more expensive than verifying one — while proposing K tokens from the draft is much cheaper than running the target K times.

attrs.alt: Speculative decoding: draft model proposes tokens, target model verifies in one parallel pass

content.text: The three-phase loop repeats until the full output is generated:
The draft model runs K autoregressive steps to produce K candidate tokens d1…dK. It is typically 10–20× smaller than the target and proportionally faster per token.

The target model processes [prompt + d1 + … + dK] in one forward pass, producing probabilities p_target(tok | context) at all K+1 positions simultaneously. This costs roughly the same as one target-model decode step regardless of K.

For each draft token di, accept it with probability min(1, p_target(di) / p_draft(di)). The first rejection terminates the run. One bonus token is always generated from the target distribution at the rejection point, so even a run where all drafts are rejected still produces one token — the baseline.

. The rejection-sampling procedure guarantees that the output distribution equals the target model distribution exactly, as if speculative decoding were never used. The only thing that changes is speed.
The acceptance rate α (fraction of draft tokens accepted) is the key metric. The expected number of tokens output per target forward pass is:

When α = 1.0 (all drafts accepted), this equals K+1 (K draft tokens plus the bonus token). When α = 0.0 (all rejected), this equals 1 (only the bonus token). Practical values of α for a well-matched draft model on English text are 0.7–0.9, giving 2–4× speedup at K = 4.

def expected_tokens_per_pass(alpha: float, K: int) -> float:
    """
    Expected tokens accepted per target forward pass under draft-model speculative decoding.
    Includes the +1 bonus token from rejection sampling.
    """
    if alpha >= 1.0:
        return K + 1
    return (1 - alpha ** (K + 1)) / (1 - alpha)


def speedup_estimate(
    alpha: float,
    K: int,
    draft_cost: float = 0.05,   # fraction of target cost per draft step
) -> float:
    """
    Approximate wall-clock speedup vs pure target autoregressive decoding.

    Denominator: 1 target pass + K draft passes (in series before the verify step)
    Numerator: expected tokens accepted per cycle
    """
    tokens_out = expected_tokens_per_pass(alpha, K)
    cost_per_cycle = 1.0 + K * draft_cost   # 1 target + K draft passes
    return tokens_out / cost_per_cycle


# Sweep acceptance rate for K=4, draft at 5% of target cost
for alpha in [0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 0.95]:
    sp = speedup_estimate(alpha, K=4, draft_cost=0.05)
    tok = expected_tokens_per_pass(alpha, K=4)
    print(f"α={alpha:.2f}  E[tokens]={tok:.2f}  speedup≈{sp:.2f}×")

# α=0.50  E[tokens]=1.97  speedup≈1.79×
# α=0.70  E[tokens]=2.87  speedup≈2.61×
# α=0.80  E[tokens]=3.36  speedup≈3.06×
# α=0.90  E[tokens]=3.91  speedup≈3.56×
# α=0.95  E[tokens]=4.23  speedup≈3.85×
The draft model must share the same tokeniser as the target model — the token IDs must match exactly so the acceptance ratio is well-defined. Beyond that, the ideal draft model is:
10–20× fewer parameters than the target. For Llama-3-70B, a good draft is Llama-3-8B or a purpose-built small model.
If the draft proposes tokens the target rarely predicts, α is low and speedup collapses. Models from the same training family with the same vocabulary tend to have high alignment.
Llama-3-8B drafts well for Llama-3-70B. Mistral-7B drafts poorly for Llama-3-70B because their probability distributions diverge.
vLLM supports draft-model speculative decoding via the
flag. Both models are loaded on the same GPU; the draft model's KV-cache is managed separately:

# Llama-3-70B target with Llama-3-8B draft, 4 speculative tokens
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct \
    --speculative-model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --num-speculative-tokens 4 \
    --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90

# Smaller setup: 8B target with 1B draft
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --speculative-model meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct \
    --num-speculative-tokens 5 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.88
The API interface is unchanged — clients still call
with the target model name. Speculative decoding is transparent to the caller.
Implementing Draft-Model Speculative Decoding from Scratch
The core loop is straightforward to implement with any two models that share a tokeniser:

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

def load_models(
    target_name: str,
    draft_name: str,
    device: str = "cuda",
):
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(target_name)
    target = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        target_name, torch_dtype=torch.float16
    ).to(device).eval()
    draft = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        draft_name, torch_dtype=torch.float16
    ).to(device).eval()
    return tok, target, draft


@torch.no_grad()
def speculative_generate(
    tokenizer,
    target_model,
    draft_model,
    prompt: str,
    max_new_tokens: int = 200,
    K: int = 4,
    temperature: float = 1.0,
) -> str:
    input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(target_model.device)
    generated = input_ids.clone()

    while generated.shape[1] - input_ids.shape[1] < max_new_tokens:
        # === Phase 1: Draft K tokens ===
        draft_ids = generated.clone()
        draft_probs = []
        for _ in range(K):
            out = draft_model(draft_ids)
            logits = out.logits[:, -1, :] / temperature
            probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            tok = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            draft_probs.append(probs[0, tok.item()].item())
            draft_ids = torch.cat([draft_ids, tok], dim=1)

        # === Phase 2: Verify with target (one pass over K+1 positions) ===
        target_out = target_model(draft_ids)
        target_logits = target_out.logits[:, generated.shape[1] - 1 : -1, :] / temperature
        target_probs_all = torch.softmax(target_logits, dim=-1)  # (1, K, vocab)

        # === Phase 3: Accept/Reject ===
        accepted = 0
        new_tokens = []
        for i in range(K):
            draft_token = draft_ids[0, generated.shape[1] + i].item()
            p_target = target_probs_all[0, i, draft_token].item()
            p_draft = draft_probs[i]
            acceptance_prob = min(1.0, p_target / max(p_draft, 1e-10))
            if torch.rand(1).item() < acceptance_prob:
                new_tokens.append(draft_token)
                accepted += 1
            else:
                # Resample from adjusted target distribution
                adjusted = torch.clamp(
                    target_probs_all[0, i] - draft_model(generated).logits[0, -1].softmax(-1),
                    min=0,
                )
                adjusted /= adjusted.sum()
                bonus = torch.multinomial(adjusted, 1).item()
                new_tokens.append(bonus)
                break

        # Always add at least the bonus token if all K accepted
        if accepted == K:
            bonus_logits = target_out.logits[0, -1, :] / temperature
            bonus_probs = torch.softmax(bonus_logits, dim=-1)
            bonus = torch.multinomial(bonus_probs, 1).item()
            new_tokens.append(bonus)

        # Append accepted tokens
        new_tensor = torch.tensor(new_tokens, device=generated.device).unsqueeze(0)
        generated = torch.cat([generated, new_tensor], dim=1)

        if tokenizer.eos_token_id in new_tokens:
            break

    return tokenizer.decode(generated[0, input_ids.shape[1]:], skip_special_tokens=True)
The optimal K balances two effects:

More tokens accepted per target pass when α is high, increasing throughput. But verification cost stays approximately constant (one target forward pass), while draft cost grows linearly — so there is a diminishing return curve.
Lower draft overhead, and failures are cheaper (fewer wasted draft steps). Better when α is moderate (0.6–0.7).

# Empirically find optimal K for your workload
import time

def benchmark_speculative_k(
    tokenizer, target, draft,
    test_prompts: list[str],
    k_values: list[int] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8],
    max_new_tokens: int = 128,
) -> dict[int, float]:
    results = {}
    for k in k_values:
        start = time.perf_counter()
        total_tokens = 0
        for prompt in test_prompts:
            out = speculative_generate(tokenizer, target, draft, prompt,
                                       max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens, K=k)
            total_tokens += len(tokenizer(out).input_ids)
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        results[k] = total_tokens / elapsed    # tokens per second
        print(f"K={k}: {results[k]:.1f} tok/s")
    return results
Verify draft and target use identical tokeniser vocab
Use a code-specialised draft (e.g. CodeLlama-7B for CodeLlama-34B)
CUDA out of memory at startup
Draft model too large; K too high; α too low — revert to standard decoding
Ensure rejection sampling uses correct adjusted distribution (not greedy)

Acceptance rate α varies by workload. Code generation typically has high α (predictable patterns) while creative writing or multilingual content can drop α below 0.6. Monitor it continuously and fall back to standard decoding when it becomes uneconomical:

import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Iterator

@dataclass
class SpecDecStats:
    total_tokens: int = 0
    total_draft_tokens: int = 0
    total_accepted: int = 0
    total_target_passes: int = 0
    total_wall_time_s: float = 0.0

    @property
    def acceptance_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.total_draft_tokens == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.total_accepted / self.total_draft_tokens

    @property
    def tokens_per_target_pass(self) -> float:
        if self.total_target_passes == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.total_tokens / self.total_target_passes

    @property
    def throughput_tps(self) -> float:
        if self.total_wall_time_s == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.total_tokens / self.total_wall_time_s

    def log(self) -> None:
        print(
            f"SpecDec | alpha={self.acceptance_rate:.3f} | "
            f"tok/pass={self.tokens_per_target_pass:.2f} | "
            f"throughput={self.throughput_tps:.1f} tok/s"
        )


def speculative_generate_tracked(
    tokenizer,
    target_model,
    draft_model,
    prompt: str,
    stats: SpecDecStats,
    K: int = 4,
    max_new_tokens: int = 200,
) -> str:
    """speculative_generate that accumulates stats into a shared SpecDecStats object."""
    import torch
    input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(target_model.device)
    generated = input_ids.clone()
    t0 = time.perf_counter()

    while generated.shape[1] - input_ids.shape[1] < max_new_tokens:
        # Draft K tokens
        draft_ids = generated.clone()
        draft_probs_list = []
        for _ in range(K):
            out = draft_model(draft_ids)
            probs = torch.softmax(out.logits[:, -1, :], dim=-1)
            tok = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            draft_probs_list.append(probs[0, tok.item()].item())
            draft_ids = torch.cat([draft_ids, tok], dim=1)

        stats.total_draft_tokens += K

        # Verify with target
        target_out = target_model(draft_ids)
        target_probs_all = torch.softmax(
            target_out.logits[:, generated.shape[1] - 1:-1, :], dim=-1
        )
        stats.total_target_passes += 1

        # Accept/reject
        accepted = 0
        new_tokens = []
        for i in range(K):
            tok_id = draft_ids[0, generated.shape[1] + i].item()
            p_t = target_probs_all[0, i, tok_id].item()
            p_d = draft_probs_list[i]
            if torch.rand(1).item() < min(1.0, p_t / max(p_d, 1e-10)):
                new_tokens.append(tok_id)
                accepted += 1
            else:
                # bonus from adjusted distribution
                adj = torch.clamp(target_probs_all[0, i] - torch.tensor(draft_probs_list[i]), min=0)
                adj = adj / adj.sum()
                new_tokens.append(torch.multinomial(adj, 1).item())
                break

        if accepted == K:
            bonus_p = torch.softmax(target_out.logits[0, -1, :], dim=-1)
            new_tokens.append(torch.multinomial(bonus_p, 1).item())

        stats.total_accepted += accepted
        new_tensor = torch.tensor(new_tokens, device=generated.device).unsqueeze(0)
        generated = torch.cat([generated, new_tensor], dim=1)
        stats.total_tokens += len(new_tokens)

        if tokenizer.eos_token_id in new_tokens:
            break

    stats.total_wall_time_s += time.perf_counter() - t0
    return tokenizer.decode(generated[0, input_ids.shape[1]:], skip_special_tokens=True)

Speculative decoding is harmful (slower than baseline) when α is very low and the draft model is not negligibly cheap. A production wrapper should measure α over a rolling window and switch to standard decoding when the effective throughput drops below a threshold:

Draft-model speculative decoding and continuous batching interact in a subtle way. In standard continuous batching, all sequences in a batch take one decode step per iteration. With speculative decoding, sequences can produce a variable number of tokens per iteration (1 to K+1), which makes batch scheduling more complex.
vLLM handles this by running speculative decoding at the batch level: the draft model proposes K tokens for

sequence in the batch simultaneously, then the target model verifies all proposals in one parallel pass. This keeps the GPU busy (large effective batch for the verify step) while still benefiting from reduced target-model calls per token.
small batch, long outputs, high α. The verify step is cheap relative to K draft steps, and each target pass produces many tokens.

very large batch. Each verification already saturates the GPU, so the reduced-pass benefit of speculative decoding is marginal. At batch size > 64, standard decoding often matches or beats speculative decoding in throughput.
speculative decoding gives the most benefit at low batch sizes (interactive/realtime use cases), not batch inference.

Speculative decoding with a draft model accelerates LLM generation by running K cheap draft steps in series and verifying all K proposals with one expensive target forward pass. The output distribution is identical to the target model's — quality is never traded for speed. The key success factor is acceptance rate α: choose a draft model from the same family as the target (same tokeniser, similar pre-training) and tune K to 4–6 for typical acceptance rates of 0.7–0.85, yielding 2–3.5x real-world speedup. In vLLM, enable it with
